article.title: Pentagon and Linux Foundation Launch OCUDU, an Open-Source Software Stack Aiming to Become the 'Linux of RAN' for 5G and 6G
article.summary: The OCUDU Ecosystem Foundation unites 47 organizations under the Linux Foundation to build an open-source radio access network stack backed by the Pentagon's FutureG office, with code due on GitHub in April.
article.body_markdown:
## Overview

The Linux Foundation announced the formation of the Open Centralized Unit Distributed Unit (OCUDU) Ecosystem Foundation at Mobile World Congress 2026 in Barcelona on March 1. The initiative, seeded by the U.S. Department of Defense's FutureG office through the National Spectrum Consortium, aims to produce a production-ready, open-source radio access network (RAN) software stack for 5G and early 6G deployments. The first version of the codebase is scheduled to be published on GitHub in April 2026, according to the [Linux Foundation's announcement](https://www.linuxfoundation.org/press/linux-foundation-announces-ocudu-ecosystem-foundation-to-accelerate-open-source-ai-ran-innovation).

The project's backers describe it as an effort to do for wireless infrastructure what Linux did for the internet and Kubernetes did for cloud computing.

## What We Know

Nine premier founding members have committed to the foundation: AMD, AT&T, DeepSig, Ericsson, Nokia, NVIDIA, SoftBank, Software Radio Systems (SRS), and Verizon. An additional 21 general members, including Cisco, Red Hat, T-Mobile, Booz Allen Hamilton, Marvell, and Keysight Technologies, have also joined. Seventeen research institutions round out the roster, among them Georgia Tech, Rice University, UC San Diego, Northeastern University, the University of Texas at Austin, Idaho National Laboratory, SRI International, and the MITRE Corporation, as reported by [PR Newswire](https://www.prnewswire.com/news-releases/linux-foundation-announces-ocudu-ecosystem-foundation-to-accelerate-open-source-ai-ran-innovation-302700303.html).

The initial software was built by DeepSig and SRS under a contract awarded by the National Spectrum Consortium in September 2025. Over the past six months, the two companies have developed a functioning, downloadable RAN stack based on the srsRAN codebase. Version 1.0 will transition the code to a BSD 3-Clause license and place it under Linux Foundation governance. Subsequent releases are planned on six-month cycles over the next 2.5 years, with features like massive MIMO support, non-terrestrial network integration, and GPU acceleration on the roadmap.

The stack is designed to run on commercial off-the-shelf hardware across Intel, AMD, and NVIDIA architectures, lowering the barrier for smaller vendors and startups to enter the RAN market without building a full software stack from scratch.

Tom Rondeau, principal director of the Pentagon's FutureG office, said that shifting maintenance to collaborative open-source governance would enable partners to "focus their resources on innovative and monetizable technologies," according to the [Linux Foundation](https://www.linuxfoundation.org/press/linux-foundation-announces-ocudu-ecosystem-foundation-to-accelerate-open-source-ai-ran-innovation). Arpit Joshipura, general manager of networking, edge, and IoT at the Linux Foundation, stated that the effort is "building an open, trusted platform" by aligning global stakeholders under a single governance framework.

Separately, NVIDIA has been open-sourcing its own Aerial software suite for AI-native 6G development, including the CUDA-Accelerated RAN toolkit, a new Aerial Framework, and an Omniverse Digital Twin for over-the-air testing. The CUDA-Accelerated RAN and Aerial Framework became available on GitHub under the Apache 2.0 license in December 2025, with the Omniverse Digital Twin following in March 2026, as detailed on the [NVIDIA blog](https://blogs.nvidia.com/blog/open-source-aerial-ai-native-6g/). These tools complement the OCUDU stack by giving researchers GPU-accelerated prototyping capabilities alongside the foundation's reference RAN implementation.

## What We Don't Know

The foundation has not disclosed specific funding figures beyond noting the initial investment from the National Spectrum Consortium and FutureG office. It remains unclear how long that government funding will continue or whether commercial members will be expected to assume the full cost of development after the initial three-year project window.

While the project's stated goal is a "minimum viable carrier-grade network solution" within three years, no telecom operator has publicly committed to deploying OCUDU software in a production network. The gap between a reference implementation and carrier-grade reliability in live networks is historically significant, and it is uncertain how quickly the open-source stack can close it.

The competitive implications for established RAN vendors like Ericsson and Nokia, both of whom are founding members, are also unclear. Open-sourcing the CU/DU layer could commoditize a portion of their current business, and how these companies plan to differentiate in an open-source RAN ecosystem has not been detailed.

## Analysis

The OCUDU project represents the most ambitious attempt yet to open-source the radio access network layer of mobile telecommunications. Previous efforts like O-RAN Alliance specifications and the O-RAN Software Community laid groundwork for interoperability, but OCUDU goes further by delivering a complete, downloadable software stack rather than just interface specifications.

The Pentagon's involvement is strategic. By funding the initial development and then handing governance to the Linux Foundation, the FutureG office is applying a model that worked for other defense-adjacent technologies. The military benefits from a diverse, competitive supplier ecosystem for wireless infrastructure, reducing dependence on any single vendor, while the broader industry gains a shared platform that lowers barriers to innovation.

The participation of both Nokia and Ericsson as founding members is notable. These are the two largest Western RAN vendors, and their willingness to support an open-source alternative to their proprietary stacks suggests they see greater risk in being excluded from the ecosystem than in potential commoditization. It may also reflect a pragmatic calculation that shaping the open-source standard is preferable to competing against it.

With 47 organizations and 17 research institutions already committed before a single line of code is public, OCUDU enters April's GitHub launch with significant momentum. Whether it can translate that early coalition into sustained engineering contributions and eventual production deployments will determine if it truly becomes the "Linux of RAN" its founders envision.
